front: Figure: legendarily cut through the Gordian Knot at Gordium, fulfilling a prophecy that its undoer would rule Asia
Figure: gave elaborate funerals to his horse Bucephalus and his dog Peritas
Figure: led an elite shock cavalry force called the Companions, typically armed with the xyston lance
Figure: after conquering Persia, required subjects to kneel and kiss his feet, a custom called proskynesis
Figure: at a mass wedding at Susa, married off many of his generals to Persian noblewomen and paid off 20,000 talents of his soldiers' debts
Figure: married the Bactrian princess Roxane after conquering Bactria
Figure: Aristotle agreed to tutor him only on condition that Aristotle's hometown Stagira be rebuilt
Figure: as a student of Aristotle, allegedly told the philosopher Diogenes of Sinope to move out of his sunlight
Figure: an apocryphal theory blamed his death on poison supplied by his old tutor Aristotle, delivered via the regent Cassander
Figure: his court historian Callisthenes, a great-nephew of Aristotle, was killed for conspiring against him with the page Hermolaus
Figure: was reportedly born after his mother Olympias's womb was struck by a lightning bolt from Zeus
Figure: a temple of Artemis at Ephesus supposedly burned down on the day of his birth
Battle: Alexander's first major battle against the Persian Empire (334 BC), fought against satraps commanded by the Greek mercenary general Memnon of Rhodes
Battle: here, Alexander's officer Cleitus the Black saved his life by severing the arm of the Persian noble Spithridates; years later Alexander killed Cleitus in a drunken quarrel
Battle: Alexander besieged this island city (in modern Lebanon) by building a massive causeway that permanently transformed it into a peninsula
Battle: before this siege, Alexander dreamed of a satyr, which his seers interpreted as 'sa Tyros' ('Tyre is yours')
Battle: this siege followed the Battle of Issus and preceded the Siege of Gaza, ending after six months when besiegers breached the city's legendary high walls
Battle: fought near the Pinarus River (333 BC) after Alexander marched through the Pass of Jonah, while Darius III executed wounded Macedonians left behind at a nearby city
Battle: Darius III lost his nerve and fled this battlefield after Alexander led a direct cavalry charge with the Companions
Figure: used the Persian Royal Road, passing through the Cilician Gates, to rapidly mobilize troops during his invasion of Persia
Battle: here, javelin-throwing Agrianian skirmishers disrupted Darius III's scythed chariots after the battlefield had been cleared of vegetation for their charge
Battle: this decisive victory over Darius III (331 BC) effectively ended the Achaemenid Empire; the satrap Bessus then killed Darius and was executed a year later at Ecbatana
Battle: following this victory (along with the earlier Battle of Issus), the Persian capital Persepolis was burned
Event: traveled to the Oracle of Zeus-Ammon at the Siwa Oasis in Egypt, where he was told he was the son of the god Ammon
back: Alexander the Great's claim to divinity
work: Claim to Divinity & the Oracle of Siwa

front: Event: his mother Olympias reportedly recounted dreams of a lightning bolt striking her and of a snake in her bed, used to support belief in his divine parentage
back: Alexander the Great's claim to divinity
work: Claim to Divinity & the Oracle of Siwa

front: Event: the philosopher Anaxarchus argued for his divinity in a debate; an opponent quipped that he would 'go away one kiss the poorer'
back: Alexander the Great's claim to divinity
work: Claim to Divinity & the Oracle of Siwa

front: Event: was often depicted with two horns due to conflation with the ram-horned god Amun
back: Alexander the Great's claim to divinity
work: Claim to Divinity & the Oracle of Siwa

front: Event: besieged the 'birdless rock' Aornos (at the spur of Pir Sar), reportedly attempting to outdo the legendary feat of Hercules
work: Indian Campaign & the Battle of the Hydaspes

front: Battle: fought along the modern-day Jhelum River (326 BC); Alexander defeated King Porus by using pikemen to drive Porus's own war elephants amok, and his horse Bucephalus died of wounds sustained here
work: Indian Campaign & the Battle of the Hydaspes

front: Battle: after defeating King Porus here, Alexander allowed him to continue ruling as a subject king, reportedly because Porus asked to be treated 'as a king would treat another king'
work: Indian Campaign & the Battle of the Hydaspes

front: Battle: Alexander founded the now-lost city of Nicaea to commemorate this victory
work: Indian Campaign & the Battle of the Hydaspes

front: Event: his exhausted army mutinied and refused to cross the Beas (Hyphasis) River, ending his campaign in India
work: Indian Campaign & the Battle of the Hydaspes

front: Event: his general Coenus urged the campaign's end at the Beas River and died shortly afterward
work: Indian Campaign & the Battle of the Hydaspes

front: Event: was shot in the lung by an arrow near the campaign's end while fighting an alliance of the Oxydracae and Malli
work: Indian Campaign & the Battle of the Hydaspes

front: Event: the costly return march from India crossed the Gedrosian Desert
work: Indian Campaign & the Battle of the Hydaspes

front: Event: died in Babylon in June 323 BC at age 32 after a mysterious illness lasting several days
Event: a Babylonian astronomer's diary recorded the day of this event simply as 'the king died'
Event: shortly before his death, gave his signet ring to his general Perdiccas
Event: after his death, his half-brother Arrhidaeus and his unborn son by Roxane were made joint successors
Event: a persistent legend held that the poison which killed him could only be carried in the hollowed-out hoof of a mule, since no other vessel could hold it
Event: the gymnosophist Calanus reportedly predicted his death shortly before self-immolating
Event: some apocryphal accounts blamed the regent Antipater or his son Cassander for the death, allegedly using poison supplied by Aristotle
Event: Darius III's mother Sisygambis starved herself to death upon hearing news of this event
Event: his body was later claimed to be preserved in honey inside a sarcophagus
Event: researcher Katherine Hall theorized the body's apparent lack of decomposition could be explained by a paralytic illness linked to C. jejuni bacteria, rather than true death
Event: his general Ptolemy I Soter seized his funeral cortege en route to Aegae and diverted it to Egypt, triggering the First War of the Diadochi
Event: his generals divided his empire at the Partition of Babylon, with Perdiccas serving as regent; these generals became known as the Diadochi ('successors')
Event: his son Alexander IV and wife Roxane were eventually poisoned on the orders of Cassander (via Glaucias), ending the Argead dynasty
Battle: fought in 301 BC in Phrygia, this battle ended Antigonus the One-Eyed's attempt to reunite Alexander's empire, killing him
Event: his generals Ptolemy I and Seleucus I founded the Ptolemaic and Seleucid dynasties, respectively, out of his former empire
Work: this Greek-language romance, attributed to Pseudo-Callisthenes, became the source for a wide medieval genre depicting Alexander's legendary exploits
Work: in this tradition, Alexander's cook Andreas accidentally discovers and uses the Water of Life while reviving a fish in the Land of Darkness, though Alexander himself fails to attain immortality
Work: legends in this tradition depict Alexander exploring the top of the world carried by griffins and the bottom of the ocean inside a glass submarine or diving bell
Work: this tradition identifies Alexander with the Quranic figure Dhu'l-Qarnayn ('the Two-Horned One'), who built a great wall to imprison the peoples Gog and Magog until Judgment Day
Work: in Ferdowsi's Persian epic the Shahnameh, Alexander is depicted as the half-brother of Darius through his mother Nahid
Work: an Egyptian legend makes Alexander the son of the exiled pharaoh Nectanebo II, who disguised himself as the god Ammon to secretly father him with Olympias
Work: the West African Epic of Sundiata calls Alexander 'a king of gold and silver' and compares its hero Sundiata to him
Work: discovered in the House of the Faun at Pompeii, this Roman floor mosaic depicts Alexander battling Darius III as a nobleman is impaled defending the fleeing Persian king
Work: in this mosaic, Alexander appears helmetless with a breastplate decorated with the head of Medusa; it is thought to be based on a lost painting by Philoxenus of Eretria
Work: this mosaic's composition leaves only a single leafless tree visible in the background, with a dying soldier's face reflected in his own round shield
Painting: commissioned by Duke William IV of Bavaria after the 1529 Siege of Vienna, this work by Albrecht Altdorfer depicts Alexander's victory over Darius III
work: The Battle of Issus (Albrecht Altdorfer painting)

front: Painting: shows a giant Latin inscription on a tablet floating in the sky above the armies, with a crescent moon in the upper left and a sun in the upper right
work: The Battle of Issus (Albrecht Altdorfer painting)

front: Painting: this anachronistic 'world landscape' unrealistically shows both Cyprus and the Nile River, with soldiers in contemporary 16th-century armor
work: The Battle of Issus (Albrecht Altdorfer painting)

front: Painting: is one of five paintings from the same cycle commissioned by William IV of Bavaria, today displayed together in Munich's Alte Pinakothek
work: The Battle of Issus (Albrecht Altdorfer painting)

front: Painting: legend holds Alexander commissioned the painter Apelles to paint his mistress Campaspe nude, then gifted her to Apelles after the painter fell in love with her; the scene was later depicted by Jacques-Louis David and Giovanni Battista Tiepolo
back: Apelles Painting Campaspe in the Presence of Alexander
work: Apelles' Painting Campaspe in the Presence of Alexander

front: Painting: in one version of this scene, the Farnese Hercules statue appears in the background while an African man watches the artist paint
back: Apelles Painting Campaspe in the Presence of Alexander
work: Apelles' Painting Campaspe in the Presence of Alexander

front: Sculpture: this temple-shaped sarcophagus depicts Alexander hunting lions and was discovered at the Royal Necropolis in Sidon, though it likely held the remains of the local king Abdalonymos rather than Alexander
Sculpture: features thin-necked lions carved at each corner of its pediment and an acroter decorated with woman-headed eagle figures
Event: a modern dispute between Greece and the Republic of (North) Macedonia over rights to Alexander's legacy involved the Vergina Sun flag symbol and the 1995 Interim Accord
Event: Oliver Stone shot his 2004 biopic Alexander in Thailand and Morocco rather than the Balkans, partly to avoid inflaming this dispute
